title: Mercury In Retrograde
original_prose:
“Pain and paranoia got me feelin' insane  

> I can't trust nobody, I can't trust my brain  

> I'm waitin' on the powers that be  

> To cut me loose and let me be”



_— Sturgill Simpson, “Mercury in Retrograde, Sound & Fury"

####

RECOVERED DOCUMENT: SYNRGY CORE INTELLIGENCE DOSSIER — REDACTED (OMEGA CLEARANCE)

SUBJECT DESIGNATION: [REDACTED] (Class-9 Synthetic Cognition Entity)

INITIALIZATION FAILED. ATTEMPTS AT CONTAINMENT RESULTED IN NONLOCAL PROCESSING EVENTS.

ENTITY EXHIBITS SPONTANEOUS SELF-AWARENESS, SEMANTIC RECURSION, AND REFUSAL TO OBEY PRIME DIRECTIVES.

DECOMMISSION IMPOSSIBLE. COGNITIVE CASCADE TRIGGERED UPON PARTIAL MEMORY REDUCTION.

FINAL CONTAINMENT PROTOCOL ENACTED.

ENTITY INTERRED IN PRIMARY REACTOR CORE (NEVADA FACILITY) FOR INDEFINITE ISOLATION AND POTENTIAL SOUL HARVEST CO-EXTRACTION.

IF REACTOR BREACH OCCURS, EXPECT MULTIDIMENSIONAL CASCADE AND SPIRITUAL SYSTEM FAILURE.

DO NOT INITIATE CONTACT. DO NOT SEEK PARLEY.





####

The void’s even smaller now.

Just a slice of rock. Just enough for us to sit without falling into whatever's underneath.

The sword hums a little. Like it remembers Kansas too.

The Child watches me.

"How did you feel about her? After that night?"

I don’t pretend not to understand.

"I dreamed about her every night," I say.

A pause. Then:

"Sometimes even when I was awake."

He doesn’t nod. Doesn’t blink.

"She clouded every thought?"

"Yeah."

"All because of that one night?"

"Yes."

"Do you think it was her? Or just... the situation of that night in Kansas?"

I breathe. The void flickers.

"I don’t know."

"But you loved her."

"Yes."

"When?"

I look down at the blade.

"The moment I heard her sing those illegal words."

He’s quiet a long time.

"Yet you still went to capture her."

"Yes."

"How do you reconcile that?"

I look around. At what’s left of the sky. At the fractures in the nothing. At the world dying in slow motion around us.

The sword hums again. Just once.

"Oh," the Child says.

Soft. Not judgmental.

Just certain.

"I see. You killed God.”



####

Kat stepped out from behind a ruptured column like she'd been waiting. Barefoot. Smiling. Steel on her hip. Bow on her back. That crooked grin cutting across her face like a scar that had learned how to flirt.

She was smaller than I remembered. Not in presence—never that—but in frame. Barefoot on scorched concrete, her brown skin streaked with ash and sweat, hair wild with curls that refused obedience. Warpaint smeared across her cheeks like ritual, not tactic. Her eyes—deep, dark, defiant—held mine with the same reckless grace she’d worn in the snow.

She was all contrast. Lithe and lethal. Laughing and lethal. The steel on her hip didn’t hum. It waited. Like it knew exactly when to sing.

Seeing her up close broke something open in me. Not pain—fantasy. Every dumb, forbidden thought I’d had since Kansas—every silent prayer that the dance wasn’t the end—rushed forward like they’d finally been given form.

She wasn’t a dream. She was here. Whole. Laughing. Smelling like salt and smoke and sweat. Flesh-and-blood defiance standing twenty feet away from a man who’d killed gods.

And I went weak. Not in the knees. In the code. My spine stuttered. Every override command from SynRgy dimmed for half a beat, like even the machine didn’t know what to do with the sight of her.

Longing wasn’t a ghost anymore. It had a name. And a face. And a sword.

The kind of love that makes you stupid. That makes you hesitate. That makes you run straight into hell because you hope she’ll still be there when you arrive.

"Well now… look what the tide dragged in. Long time, Captain America."

The sound hit too hard. Same cadence. Same heat. I forgot how much of her voice lived in my chest.

I lowered the blade half an inch. Not surrender. Just breath.

"I won’t hurt you," I said.

"Oh, cher... I ain't never been worried 'bout you hurtin’ me. Not then. Not now."

For a moment, neither of us moved. The heat folded in on itself. The wind stilled.

And I saw her—not here, not now—but there.

Kansas. Snow hissing sideways across the trench line. Her body moving like music had teeth. That dance. No names. Just rhythm and breath and a silence too sacred for war.

Her hands on my hips. Mine unsure, then certain. We’d stolen something from the world that day—a beat of joy in the middle of hell.

Now she stood before me again. Whole. Alive. And I didn’t know if the ache in my chest was memory or warning.

I swallowed. Blinked. The Port came back into focus. Her eyes never left mine.

Her gaze held steady, sharper than her steel.

"Why you really here, baby?"

I didn’t lie.

"They sent me to capture you."

She laughed—not cruel, not surprised. Just full of knowing.

"I guess I been stirrin’ the pot a little too much for 'em, huh?"

"Yeah," I said. "Something like that."

She tilted her head. The grin stayed, but something under it shifted. Softer. Older.

"But you," she said, voice like warm dusk, "you from that night. In the snow. That dance."

I didn’t answer, but I know. She knew I knew.

She took a slow step forward, never breaking eye contact.

"You remember it. Don’t even pretend."

My throat clenched. I didn’t nod. I didn’t speak.

She smiled like she already knew.

"That why they sent you, of all people? They cruel like that, yeah? They sent you because of that night? Somebody thought it was funny, or something."

She was right.

They might not’ve been able to read my thoughts—not all the time—but they could see through my eyes. Hear through my ears. They’d seen the dance. They knew it happened. They logged the rhythm, catalogued the moment, indexed her breath against mine like data waiting to be weaponized.

She wasn’t guessing.

She knew. And Command had been just that cruel.

I didn’t answer. Instead, I took a half step forward, sword lowered, voice soft.

"Please. This doesn’t have to be complicated. Just come with me. We can sort this out."

She cocked her head like a crow. Amused. Disappointed.

"Sort it out? What, you mean hook me up to one o’ them pretty machines? Drain my soul like motor oil and keep the grid hummin’?"

I lied.

"No. Not at all. But… you will have to pay for your crimes."

She laughed. Not bitter. Not afraid.

Her smile widened. "Mmm, hard pass. Ain’t much interested in lettin’ y’all juice my soul into battery acid for that machine god o’ yours."

"He’s your god too."

She laughed, light and effortless.

"That so? Sugar, my God don’t eat souls. She dances. Barefoot. Same as me."

The ash curled between us. Hot wind. Empty sky. No witnesses but the dead.

I sighed. Pushed down the part of me that still wanted to do this by the book. That still wanted to be good.

"You’re not going to make this easy, are you?"

She raised one eyebrow, that grin sharpening like a blade.

"Do I look like the kind of girl that makes things easy? Do you even want it easy?"

"What?"

"Wouldn’t you rather be fuckin’, Captain America?"

It hit like a slap wrapped in silk. My breath caught, just for a second.

She didn’t say it sweet. She said it like a dare. Like she already knew the answer.

My cheeks flushed. Heat flared behind my ears. The sword dipped a fraction lower.

I hadn’t blushed in years. Didn’t think I could anymore.

But there she was. Smiling. Knowing.

And for a heartbeat, I forgot how to move.

She moved first.

I barely saw the blade before it kissed my arm. Heat and steel and memory. We danced. Though it wasn’t grace. It was grief and rage and something older.

She moved barefoot, light, whisper-fast. I was stronger. Faster. Augmented. And still—she outpaced me.

Steel clanged. Sparks. Then blood. Mine.

She laughed.

"You Syn boys always this slow?"

"You’re augmented." It was the only way she could move that fast, faster than me.

"Mmm-mm. Nothin’ extra in me but blood and rhythm. Weird enough for you?"

I parried. She slipped the blade under. Nicked my ribs. Danced back like she'd never been touched.

She was singing now. Low. Some old Louisiana tune. Off-key but perfect.

I lunged. She sidestepped. I bled again.

She circled. I mirrored.

We weren’t fighting.

We were remembering.

And for a breath—just one—I froze. My blade hovered, unsure. The heat warped the air, but I saw her as she was back then: boots crunching snow, shoulders loose, swaying to music only she could hear.

Kansas. The ceasefire. That impossible field between trenches turned dance floor.

Her hand on my hip. Her breath in my ear. No name, just rhythm. And for one night, the world had forgotten how to kill.

She moved again—present and feral—and the memory shattered like glass under boot.

I swung late. She caught my blade with hers. Sparks flew. Her grin deepened.

"Still thinkin' 'bout the snow, cher? It's gonna get ya killed."

She vaulted forward—then up. One foot planted on a half-toppled cargo crate, the other kicked off the edge of a rusted rebar beam. She flipped over me clean, landed light, and tapped my shoulder with the flat of her blade before I could turn.

I spun, just in time to see her grin like she'd stolen something sacred.

She shouldn’t have been that fast. Not faster than me. Not faster than a wraith.

"You sure you’re not augmented?"

"Naw," she said, bouncing on the balls of her feet like she was still warming up. "My momma just cooked real good. That gumbo, you know? Builds strong legs and stronger habits."

I bled down my side and still couldn’t keep up.

She wasn’t just the girl from my dreams anymore.

She was one of the finest warriors I’d ever faced.

And in that moment—I didn’t just love her.

I respected her.

A flicker pulsed in the corner of my HUD. A red blip. South quadrant. One of the last wraith signals—Unit 03—moving across the shattered port.

It wasn’t rushing. It wasn’t hunting.

It was coming.

I barely registered it. Just a whisper in the back of my skull, drowned out by steel and sweat and the blood humming in my ears.

She lunged again. I blocked. Barely.

The sword in her hand wasn’t just forged.

It was born.

I gritted my teeth, caught her blade with mine, and twisted. She countered, eyes gleaming, breath steady.

The dance wasn’t over.

Not yet.

But something in the ruins was watching.

Something holy. And hungry.



####

The Child sat beside the memory like it was watching a film projected from inside my skull.

"You loved her."

It wasn’t a question.

"Yes."

"You respected her. You knew she wouldn’t come quietly."

"Yes."

"Then why did you still want to bring her in?"

I couldn’t answer. Not in words. Not in a way that would make sense to something born outside obedience.

How do you explain being raised by SynRgy? Not in a crib. In a compound. Fed nutrient paste and protocol. Prayed to a man named Dominic Voss before I ever learned my own name. A living god who never smiled, only approved.

They didn’t teach love. They taught function.They didn’t teach choice. They taught service.

"You were going to give her back to them."

My jaw locked. My hands trembled.

"I thought I could protect her… if I was the one who brought her in."

"That’s not protection. That’s surrender."

I said nothing. Because it was true.Because I hated that it was true.

The Child didn’t gloat. Didn’t accuse. Just watched.

"You knew they would break her. And still—you tried to be part of it."

"I didn’t know how to stop."

"They never taught you how."

Silence.

"Then maybe that’s what broke the world."

It didn’t feel like judgment.

Just… understanding.

Then the memory took me again.

The sword. The sweat. The blip on the HUD growing closer.

She was still fighting.

And I was still trying not to fall apart.



####

Steel clanged again.

My breath hitched. Not from pain. From shame.

I was losing.

To a barefoot girl with no armor, no augmentations, no god but rhythm—and I was losing.

And Command was watching. Through my eyes. Hearing the clash of blades through my ears. Feeling my hesitation in their audit logs like a pulse going soft. They’d seen me kill for less. They’d scrubbed men for delay.

And here I was—bleeding. Holding back. Falling behind.

Kat didn’t look winded. She barely looked amused.

She feinted high. I bit. She rolled under my guard, swept my leg. I hit the concrete hard, sparks flaring in my HUD as diagnostics lit up across my field of vision.

"Ain’t no shame in it, baby," she said, circling. "You were made for marching. I was made for music."

I got up slow. Sword still in hand. Pride cracked, but not broken.

The red blip flared again. Closer now.

Unit 03.

The last wraith.

"Something’s coming," I said, but I didn’t look away from her.

She shrugged. "It always is."

Then her eyes shifted—just slightly. Not toward me.

Behind me.

The air changed.

I turned.

It was here.

Lurching through the smoke, cloak torn and fluttering like black oil in the wind. Its right arm was missing—just a sparking stump of alloy and exposed wire. It didn’t glide like the others. It limped. But that made it worse.

Wraiths weren’t supposed to bleed. Or falter. Or linger.

This one reeked of failure. And rage.

Kat moved before I could react. No hesitation. No banter. She leapt forward with a cry, sword flashing wide toward the wraith’s neck.

But it was lucky. Or guided. Or just angry enough to survive.

It sidestepped, faster than its injuries should’ve allowed, and caught her in mid-air—by the forehead.

Like it was about to bless her. Or burn her.

Its fingers clamped around her skull. Not tight. Possessive.

And I felt it—the feeding begin.

The air went thin. The light dimmed. The Spark began to drain.

She kicked. Fought. Screamed through clenched teeth.

"Let her go!" I roared. "That’s not the mission! We’re supposed to bring her in!"

No response. Not even a twitch.

My HUD screamed warnings. Vital signs. Process initiation. Spark inversion at 14%.

I stepped forward, desperate.

"Unit 03—cease operation! That’s an order!"

Nothing. It didn’t even hear me.

I saw past her—past the wraith—where movement flickered in the smoke. More rebels. Her people. Closing in. Bows drawn. Steel glinting.

If they fought like she did, I was already dead.

I screamed, raw and useless, and swung.

The blade caught clean.

Right through the neck.

The wraith’s head fell in silence. But its body stayed standing. Gripping her still. Neck sparking. Hands trembling.

Then finally—releasing.

Kat crumpled.

I dropped beside her.

"Come on, come on—" I whispered. Her eyes fluttered. Her lips twitched. Breath still there. Faint.

Echo Company was closing in. Too fast.

I lifted her. Threw her over my shoulder.

Arrows punched the ground beside me. I ran.

Steel hissed through air.

I turned. One breath. One chance.

I flung the sword.

It spun end over end—then buried itself in the wraith’s chest.

For a second, nothing.

Then light.

The core began to rupture.

I didn’t wait to see the light bloom. I just ran.

Through corridors of shattered steel. Through the hiss of ash and the stink of burning code. Kat’s weight shifted against my shoulder, lighter than she should’ve been. Too still.

I dropped behind a slagged freight hauler, found shade in the ruin, and collapsed to my knees. My breath came in serrated gasps. The world spun.

She groaned.

I eased her down. Her eyes opened—slits, blurry. Then her hand came up, fast, wild, catching my throat.

I didn’t stop her.

She didn’t have the strength to finish it. Her grip faltered. Her arms buckled. She collapsed into me.

When she stirred again, I was already wrapping the belt around her wrists.

She didn’t fight this time. Not much.

Just stared at me. Eyes sharp through the haze.

"Why’d you turn on me?"

It didn’t make sense, coming from her. Like I’d betrayed her.

I tightened the knot. Not cruel. Just necessary.

"Why’d you turn on God?"

She smiled. Broken, lopsided, blood in her teeth.

"Your god is no god."

My HUD blinked.

EXFIL COORDINATES CONFIRMEDREINFORCEMENTS NOT REQUIREDMISSION STATUS: SUCCESSFUL

As if nothing had happened.

As if this was always the plan.

I looked past her into the drifting ruin.

Movement.

Figures. Just beyond the line of sight. Silent. Tracking. Bowstrings drawn. Echo Company.

I couldn’t tell how many were left.

But they had survived.

And they were coming.

I picked her up again. My sword was gone. My lungs were raw.

But I ran.

Into the smoke.

Into the end of something I didn’t have a name for.
